worth_it_explanation: Account structure is the layer everything else sits on, so a structure problem distorts reporting, slows bulk edits, and muddies the data the audit relies on. The checks here are fast since they read what is already visible in Business Settings and the Ads Manager reporting table, and the fixes are organizational rather than a rebuild. Sorting out which brands and regions belong in which ad account, removing test campaigns from a production account, and adopting a name template pays off on every report and edit that follows.
intro: Account structure covers how an advertiser's ad accounts, campaigns, and naming are organized inside Meta Business Suite. A clean structure keeps unrelated brands and regions in separate ad accounts, keeps test work out of the account that holds live spend, and uses a consistent naming pattern so reports and bulk edits behave predictably. These checks confirm that the ad account is scoped to one brand or business line, that test campaigns are not mixed in with production, and that campaigns follow a naming convention the reporting table can group on.

audit_checks.title: Unrelated brands or regions share a single ad account
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When several brands or regions run inside one ad account, their spend, audiences, and pixel signals blend together, which makes reporting harder to read and access harder to scope to the right people. The campaigns still run, so the cost is in clarity and control rather than an outage.

educate.base:
In Meta Business Suite, an ad account is the container that holds campaigns, billing, and the dataset (the renamed Meta Pixel) that feeds optimization. When one ad account carries several unrelated brands or regions, their reporting, budgets, and audience signals share that single container. A report at the account level then mixes brands that should be read separately, and access granted at the account level reaches every brand inside it rather than the one a person should touch.

Meta provides structure above the ad account for exactly this reason. A business portfolio can hold multiple ad accounts, and a business asset group can organize accounts, Pages, and datasets by brand, business line, or region so people and partners are assigned only the assets they work on. Splitting unrelated brands into their own ad accounts keeps each brand's spend, billing, and signals scoped to itself.

A few signs suggest unrelated brands or regions are sharing one account:
  • Campaigns for clearly different brands or storefronts appear side by side in the same ad account
  • One account's spend report has to be filtered by name before any single brand's numbers make sense
  • A person granted access to run one brand can see and edit every other brand's campaigns
You may want to weigh the reporting and access gains of a split against the work of moving campaigns and re-establishing each account's learning. Separating accounts is an organizational change, not a tracking fix, so the decision depends on how distinct the brands or regions really are.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, separate brands or storefronts each have their own catalog and dataset, so sharing one ad account ties those catalogs and purchase signals to a single container. You may want to confirm each storefront has its own ad account so its catalog sales reporting and purchase optimization stay scoped to that store rather than blended with another brand's orders.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, separate business lines often have different lead definitions and follow-up teams, so a shared ad account blends lead volume that should be read per line. You may want to confirm each business line has its own ad account so lead reporting and access map to the team that owns the leads.

learn_more.source_title: Add an ad account to your business portfolio in Meta Business Suite

investigate.intro: Review the ad accounts in Business Settings and check whether any single account holds campaigns for more than one brand or region.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Business Settings in Meta Business Suite and select 'Accounts', then 'Ad accounts', to list the ad accounts in the business portfolio.
Open each ad account and scan the campaign names and any linked Pages or datasets to see whether the campaigns belong to one brand or several.
Note any ad account whose campaigns clearly span unrelated brands, storefronts, or regions since that is the account a split would address.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the ad account in Ads Manager and view the campaign list at the account level.
Check whether the campaigns require filtering by brand or region before the list reads as a single business, which signals more than one brand is sharing the account.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad account, whether the campaigns belong to a single brand or business line or span several. An account scoped to one brand or region passes. An account holding campaigns for unrelated brands, storefronts, or regions is a finding recorded with the account name and the brands or regions it mixes.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each ad account, whether it holds one brand or several above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs unrelated brands or regions inside a single Meta ad account. Their spend, audiences, and dataset signals share one container, so account-level reporting blends businesses that should be read separately, and account-level access reaches every brand rather than the one a person should manage. {{voice_recommend}} separating the unrelated brands or regions into their own ad accounts within the business portfolio, and using a business asset group to assign each account to the people and partners who work on it. The split keeps each brand's reporting, billing, and optimization signals scoped to itself.

audit_checks.title: Test campaigns run inside the production ad account
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Test campaigns in the live account spend real budget, add their results to account-level reporting, and feed the dataset that production optimization learns from. The numbers an audit reads then include experiments that were never meant to count, and a misconfigured test can spend before anyone notices.

educate.base:
A production ad account is the account that holds an advertiser's live spend and the reporting decisions are made from. When test campaigns, whether a quick creative experiment, a throwaway audience trial, or a half-built draft that was set live, run inside that same account, their spend and results land in the account's totals. A report read at the account level then sums production performance together with experiments that were never meant to be counted.

The effect reaches past reporting. Conversions and other events from test campaigns flow into the same dataset that production campaigns optimize against, so Advantage+ and other automated bidding learn partly from traffic the advertiser does not actually want more of. A test that targets the wrong audience or fires the wrong event teaches the account the wrong lesson while it runs.

Keeping tests out of the production account is the cleaner pattern. Some advertisers hold a separate ad account for experiments, while others contain tests behind a clear naming prefix and a hard budget cap so they can be filtered out of every report. The point is that production totals and the production dataset reflect production only.

A few signs suggest tests are running in the production account:
  • Campaigns named 'test', 'tmp', 'draft', or a person's initials sit alongside the live campaigns
  • Account-level spend includes line items no one recognizes as a real campaign
  • A campaign with an unusually small or open-ended budget is live rather than paused
You may want to decide whether each test belongs in a separate account or simply needs a naming and budget convention that keeps it out of production reporting.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a test campaign that fires the purchase event, or a test catalog set that lists real products, feeds purchase and catalog signals into the dataset that real sales campaigns optimize on. You may want to confirm test campaigns do not send the purchase event into the production dataset since a test that reports purchases trains the account on conversions you did not actually make.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a test campaign pointed at a live lead form adds test submissions to the lead count and the optimization signal. You may want to confirm tests use a separate form or are excluded from the lead event, so the lead totals and the audience the account learns from reflect real prospects.

learn_more.source_title: What are campaigns, ad sets and ads in Meta Ads Manager?

investigate.intro: Scan the production ad account's campaign list for experiments, drafts, or throwaway tests that are spending or feeding the dataset alongside the live campaigns.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the production ad account in Ads Manager and view all campaigns, including paused ones, over a date range that covers recent activity.
Look for campaigns whose names or settings mark them as tests, for example 'test', 'tmp', or 'draft' prefixes, a single ad set with a tiny budget, or initials in the name.
For each suspected test, check whether it has spent in the period and whether it sends a conversion event since spend affects reporting and events affect the dataset production campaigns optimize on.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign that reads as a test, whether it has spent in the audited period and whether it sends a conversion event into the production dataset. A production account with no test campaigns spending or sending events passes. A test campaign that spends, or that feeds a conversion event into the production dataset, is a finding recorded with the campaign name and whether the issue is spend, event contamination, or both.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each suspected test campaign, whether it spent and whether it sends a conversion event above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} test campaigns running inside {{site_name}}'s production ad account. Their spend lands in the account's totals and their events feed the dataset that live campaigns optimize against, so the reported performance includes experiments that were never meant to count, and automated bidding learns partly from traffic {{site_name}} does not want more of. {{voice_recommend}} moving experiments into a separate ad account, or at minimum holding them behind a clear naming prefix and a capped budget and excluding their events from the production dataset. That keeps the production totals and the optimization signal reflecting live campaigns only.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns have no consistent naming convention for reporting and bulk edits
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a naming pattern, the reporting table cannot group or filter campaigns reliably and automated rules that match on campaign name miss their targets, so reporting and bulk edits take longer and are easier to get wrong. Delivery is unaffected, so the cost is operational rather than performance.

educate.base:
A naming convention is a fixed pattern that every campaign, ad set, and ad name follows, built from the same fields in the same order, for example objective, audience, region, and date. The pattern is the thing the Ads Manager reporting table searches and filters on, and it is what automated rules read when they act on a campaign whose name contains a given keyword. When names are written ad hoc, with different people using different words and orders, that grouping and matching falls apart.

The practical cost shows up in two places. Reporting cannot roll campaigns up by objective or region because there is no consistent token to filter on, so the practitioner sorts by hand each time. Automated rules that target campaigns by a keyword in the name either miss campaigns that named the concept differently or catch ones that happen to share a word, which makes the rule unreliable.

Meta supports this directly through name templates in Ads Manager, which pre-fill new campaign, ad set, and ad names from chosen fields so everyone in the account follows the same pattern. Adopting a template fixes naming going forward, while existing campaigns can be renamed to match where the history is worth preserving.

A few signs point to a missing convention:
  • Two campaigns with the same objective and audience are named in completely different ways
  • The reporting table has no name token that reliably isolates a region, objective, or funnel stage
  • An automated rule keyed to a campaign name acts on the wrong campaigns or misses some
You may want to agree on a field order with whoever names campaigns before applying a template since a template only helps once everyone uses the same pattern.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, naming that encodes the catalog, funnel stage, or promotion lets the reporting table separate prospecting from retargeting and one sale period from another. You may want to confirm campaign names carry a consistent token for funnel stage so catalog sales reporting can be grouped without sorting each campaign by hand.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, naming that encodes the business line and offer lets reports group lead volume by the team that owns each line. You may want to confirm campaign names carry a consistent business-line token so lead reporting rolls up cleanly across the account.

learn_more.source_title: Create a name template in Meta Ads Manager

investigate.intro: Read the campaign and ad set names in the reporting table and check whether they follow one consistent pattern that reporting and rules can rely on.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the ad account in Ads Manager and view the campaign list, then the ad set list within a few campaigns.
Check whether names share a fixed set of fields in a fixed order, for example objective, audience, and region, or whether they vary by who created them.
Try filtering the reporting table on a token you would expect to be consistent, such as a region or funnel stage, and note whether it isolates the right campaigns or returns a mixed set.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether campaign and ad set names follow one consistent, documented pattern and whether a name template is applied in the account. Names that follow a fixed field order and support reliable filtering pass. Ad hoc names that cannot be filtered or grouped on a consistent token are a finding, noting whether a name template exists and whether existing names need renaming to match.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether campaign names follow a consistent pattern and whether a name template is applied above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s campaigns follow no consistent naming convention. The reporting table cannot group or filter campaigns on a reliable token, and automated rules that match on campaign name act on the wrong campaigns or miss some, so reporting and bulk edits take longer and are easier to get wrong. {{voice_recommend}} agreeing on a fixed field order with everyone who names campaigns, applying a name template in Ads Manager so new names follow that pattern automatically, and renaming the existing campaigns worth preserving to match. Consistent names let reporting roll up cleanly and let name-based rules act on exactly the campaigns intended.
